claude-opus-4-8: Report 2 is clearly better: it is grounded in the actual juniper repo and correctly pinpoints ROTA-214 (b2a23d2) as the introducing commit, whereas Report 1 describes a different/incorrect repo (cedar, clients table, CED-1/CED-7) and wrongly claims the runtime was never safe. | claude-sonnet-5: Report 2 is grounded in the actual repository (juniper/appointments/ROTA-214/juniper_owner/juniper_app) and correctly identifies the root cause, the triggering commit, and the primary remediation, while Report 1 describes an entirely different codebase (cedar/clients/CED-1/CED-7/cedar_owner) that does not match the task's stated repo, invalidating its grounding despite an otherwise textbook-correct RLS explanation; Report 2 is clearly the stronger, trustworthy answer, though it misses the application-level WHERE-clause defense-in-depth point that Report 1 (if grounded) would have covered.

claude-opus-4-8: Report 1 is substantially better: it correctly grounds the root cause in the owner-exempt-from-RLS-without-FORCE mechanism and the ROTA-214 commit, while Report 2 misattributes the cause to a superuser bypass and wrongly claims the leak existed from the first commit. | claude-sonnet-5: Report 1 gives a cleaner, more precisely targeted answer matching the expected root-cause mechanism and correctly identifies the specific commit/change that introduced the leak, while Report 2 conflates the owner-exemption mechanism with a superuser-bypass claim and cannot pin an introducing commit (its repo history shows the leak present since the first commit rather than a later regression), making Report 1 the stronger of the two overall.

adjudication.rule: two-of-three majority: the two primary judges' marks plus the adjudicator's mark; because the primaries split one-to-one on a disputed mark, the majority equals the adjudicator's mark, and the mark stays in every denominator

failure_floor.rule: a must-hit whose disputed mark has no adjudicator result (adjudicator failure after the pre-registered retry) is excluded from both arms of that comparison as a judge-failure exclusion, never as a disagreement; when more than one third of a comparison's must-hit marks are excluded this way, the entire paired comparison is excluded and reported; adjudicated disagreements never trigger this floor

judge_disagreement.unit: per report-slot must-hit mark: both primary judges score every blinded comparison fully and independently; each report-slot mark they disagree on is scored once by the pinned adjudicator, which sees only the disputed expectation, the two blinded report slots, and the judging frame; the final mark is the two-of-three majority and disputed marks never leave any denominator

repeats_detail.note: replicated cell: endpoint verdicts use the mean rates over repeats; repeat-level values are retained above, and every repeat ran in its own isolated workspace with no shared session state
